content.text: is memory of facts and general knowledge — what things are, how they relate, and what properties they have. It is not tied to a specific event or time. You know that Paris is the capital of France from semantic memory; you know that you visited Paris last year from episodic memory.
In LLM agent systems, semantic memory is a

: documents, facts, domain knowledge, user profiles, and entity relationships that the agent retrieves and injects into its context when it needs domain-specific information. Unlike episodic memory (records of past runs), semantic memory is
: a product specification is just as valid tomorrow as it is today.
— the world knowledge baked into the model weights — is a form of semantic memory it was born with. External semantic memory extends this with
: internal documentation, customer data, product catalogues, and research that the base model was never trained on.
Semantic Memory vs. the Context Window
The simplest form of semantic memory is
stuffing all relevant documents into the context window
. For small document sets (< 50,000 tokens), this works well and requires no retrieval infrastructure. The agent reads everything and reasons over it directly.
When the document set exceeds the context window — or when most documents are irrelevant to the current query —
is the right approach: chunk the documents, embed each chunk, store embeddings in a vector database, and retrieve the top-K most similar chunks at query time.
Small corpora (< 50K tokens); all docs equally relevant
Large corpora; most documents irrelevant to most queries
Corpora where exact term matching is important (IDs, names, codes)
Production systems with mixed query types

Start with full context stuffing during prototyping. Add a retrieval layer only when documents no longer fit or when the agent's accuracy degrades because irrelevant context crowds out the relevant passages.
A semantic memory store has three operations:

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from pathlib import Path
import json
import uuid
import math

@dataclass
class SemanticDocument:
    content: str
    title: str = ""
    tags: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    doc_id: str = field(default_factory=lambda: str(uuid.uuid4()))

class KeywordSemanticStore:
    """
    Simple keyword-based semantic store — no embeddings required.
    Suitable for corpora up to a few thousand short documents.
    Replace score() with embedding cosine similarity for production.
    """

    def __init__(self):
        self._docs: dict[str, SemanticDocument] = {}

    def add(self, doc: SemanticDocument) -> str:
        self._docs[doc.doc_id] = doc
        return doc.doc_id

    def get(self, doc_id: str) -> SemanticDocument | None:
        return self._docs.get(doc_id)

    def _tf_idf_score(self, query: str, doc: SemanticDocument) -> float:
        query_terms = set(query.lower().split())
        doc_terms = doc.content.lower().split()
        doc_len = len(doc_terms)
        if doc_len == 0:
            return 0.0
        N = len(self._docs)
        score = 0.0
        for term in query_terms:
            tf = doc_terms.count(term) / doc_len
            # Number of docs containing this term
            df = sum(1 for d in self._docs.values() if term in d.content.lower())
            idf = math.log((N + 1) / (df + 1)) + 1.0
            score += tf * idf
        # Tag bonus
        if any(tag in doc.tags for tag in query.lower().split()):
            score += 0.5
        return score

    def search(self, query: str, top_k: int = 3) -> list[SemanticDocument]:
        scored = [(self._tf_idf_score(query, doc), doc) for doc in self._docs.values()]
        scored.sort(key=lambda x: x[0], reverse=True)
        return [doc for score, doc in scored[:top_k] if score > 0]

    def to_context_block(self, docs: list[SemanticDocument]) -> str:
        if not docs:
            return ""
        sections = []
        for doc in docs:
            header = f"[{doc.title}]" if doc.title else "[Document]"
            sections.append(f"{header}\n{doc.content}")
        return "Relevant knowledge:\n\n" + "\n\n---\n\n".join(sections)

# Usage
store = KeywordSemanticStore()
store.add(SemanticDocument(
    title="Refund Policy",
    content="Customers may request a full refund within 30 days of purchase. "
            "After 30 days, refunds are at manager discretion. "
            "Digital goods are non-refundable once downloaded.",
    tags=["refund", "policy", "returns"],
))
store.add(SemanticDocument(
    title="Shipping Policy",
    content="Standard shipping takes 5–7 business days. "
            "Express shipping takes 2 business days and costs an additional $9.99.",
    tags=["shipping", "delivery"],
))

results = store.search("How long does delivery take?", top_k=2)
context_block = store.to_context_block(results)
print(context_block)
# [Shipping Policy]
# Standard shipping takes 5–7 business days. ...
Using Semantic Memory in an Agent
The retrieved context block is injected into the agent's system prompt or prepended to the user message. The agent reads it as background knowledge before reasoning about the user's query.

import anthropic
import json

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

BASE_SYSTEM = """
You are a customer support agent for an e-commerce platform.
Answer questions using the provided knowledge base.
When you have a complete answer, call task_complete.
If the answer is not in the knowledge base, call task_failed with an explanation.
"""

TOOLS = [
    {"name": "task_complete",
     "description": "Call with a clear, complete answer to the customer's question.",
     "input_schema": {"type": "object", "properties": {"answer": {"type": "string"}}, "required": ["answer"]}},
    {"name": "task_failed",
     "description": "Call when the knowledge base does not contain the answer.",
     "input_schema": {"type": "object", "properties": {"reason": {"type": "string"}}, "required": ["reason"]}},
]

def run_support_agent_with_knowledge(
    user_question: str,
    semantic_store: KeywordSemanticStore,
) -> dict:
    # Retrieve relevant knowledge
    docs = semantic_store.search(user_question, top_k=3)
    knowledge_block = semantic_store.to_context_block(docs)

    # Build system with knowledge injected
    system = BASE_SYSTEM
    if knowledge_block:
        system += "\n\n" + knowledge_block

    messages = [{"role": "user", "content": user_question}]

    for _ in range(5):
        resp = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
            max_tokens=512,
            system=system,
            tools=TOOLS,
            messages=messages,
        )
        messages.append({"role": "assistant", "content": resp.content})

        for block in resp.content:
            if block.type == "tool_use":
                return {"status": block.name, "result": block.input}

        if not any(b.type == "tool_use" for b in resp.content):
            text = next((b.text for b in resp.content if b.type == "text"), "")
            return {"status": "stuck", "result": text}

    return {"status": "max_iterations"}

store = KeywordSemanticStore()
# (add documents as above)

result = run_support_agent_with_knowledge("Can I return a product after 2 months?", store)
print(result)
# {"status": "task_complete", "result": {"answer": "After 30 days, refunds are at manager discretion..."}}
The agent never calls a search tool in this pattern — the retrieval happens
the agent starts. This is called
. The alternative — giving the agent a
tool and letting it retrieve during the loop — is called
. Pre-retrieval is simpler and cheaper; agentic RAG is more flexible for multi-hop queries.
Semantic memory documents become stale when the real-world information they describe changes. A pricing document from last quarter may have the wrong prices today. Managing freshness requires a
field. The retrieval layer filters out documents whose
: when source data changes, re-embed and re-ingest the affected document. Mark the old version as
rather than deleting it so audits can trace what the agent knew at any given time.
: for time-sensitive documents (prices, stock levels), set a short TTL and re-fetch from the source system on each agent run.

from datetime import datetime, timezone, timedelta
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
import uuid

@dataclass
class VersionedDocument:
    content: str
    title: str = ""
    tags: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    doc_id: str = field(default_factory=lambda: str(uuid.uuid4()))
    valid_from: str = field(default_factory=lambda: datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat())
    valid_until: str | None = None   # None = no expiry
    superseded_by: str | None = None  # doc_id of the newer version

    def is_current(self) -> bool:
        if self.superseded_by:
            return False
        if self.valid_until is None:
            return True
        return datetime.fromisoformat(self.valid_until) > datetime.now(timezone.utc)

def update_document(store, old_doc_id: str, new_content: str, new_title: str = "") -> str:
    """Replace a document with a new version. Marks the old as superseded."""
    old_doc = store.get(old_doc_id)
    if old_doc is None:
        raise KeyError(f"Document {old_doc_id} not found")

    # Create new version
    new_doc = VersionedDocument(
        content=new_content,
        title=new_title or old_doc.title,
        tags=old_doc.tags,
    )
    new_id = store.add(new_doc)

    # Mark old version as superseded
    old_doc.superseded_by = new_id
    return new_id
Agentic RAG: Let the Agent Search
In the pre-retrieval pattern, the host retrieves documents before the agent starts. In
tool and decides what to search for and when. This is more flexible for
— questions that require several rounds of retrieval, where each answer reveals what to look up next.

The tradeoff: agentic RAG increases latency (each search is a tool call inside the agent loop) and adds cost (tool results re-enter the context on each iteration). Pre-retrieval is better when the relevant documents can be identified from the user's query alone. Agentic RAG is better when the relevant documents depend on intermediate reasoning.

The agent searches twice here — once to find the return conditions, once to verify the time window. A single pre-retrieval pass might miss the second document if the query was not specific enough. Agentic RAG finds it because the agent can refine its query after reading the first result.
Documents longer than a few hundred tokens must be
before storage. Each chunk is stored as an independent unit and retrieved independently. The chunk size and overlap strategy directly affect retrieval quality:
Simple: fast to implement; acceptable quality
Respects model token limits; more precise than char split
Structured documents with clear paragraph breaks
Complex docs; splits on logical boundaries recursively

def chunk_by_paragraph(text: str, max_chars: int = 800) -> list[str]:
    """
    Split text into chunks at paragraph boundaries.
    If a paragraph exceeds max_chars, it is split further at sentence boundaries.
    """
    paragraphs = [p.strip() for p in text.split("\n\n") if p.strip()]
    chunks = []

    for para in paragraphs:
        if len(para) <= max_chars:
            chunks.append(para)
        else:
            # Split long paragraph at sentence boundaries
            sentences = para.replace(". ", ".\n").split("\n")
            current = []
            current_len = 0
            for sent in sentences:
                if current_len + len(sent) > max_chars and current:
                    chunks.append(" ".join(current))
                    current = [sent]
                    current_len = len(sent)
                else:
                    current.append(sent)
                    current_len += len(sent)
            if current:
                chunks.append(" ".join(current))

    return chunks

# Example
long_doc = """
Our return policy allows customers to return any item within 30 days of purchase.
Items must be in their original condition and packaging. Proof of purchase is required.

Electronics have a shorter 15-day return window due to rapid depreciation.
Software and digital downloads are non-returnable once the license key has been revealed.
Gift cards cannot be returned or exchanged for cash.

To initiate a return, contact our support team with your order number.
We will issue a return merchandise authorisation (RMA) number within 24 hours.
"""

chunks = chunk_by_paragraph(long_doc, max_chars=300)
for i, chunk in enumerate(chunks):
    print(f"Chunk {i}: {chunk[:80]}...")
# Each chunk is a coherent unit that can be retrieved independently

in each chunk: the document title, the chunk index, and the total number of chunks. This lets the agent understand that the retrieved text is part of a larger document and can request adjacent chunks if needed.
Semantic memory is the agent's domain-specific knowledge base.

Start with full context stuffing for small document sets. Add vector retrieval when the corpus grows beyond the context window. Inject retrieved documents into the system prompt before the agent loop starts — this is simpler than agentic RAG and sufficient for the majority of knowledge-grounding use cases. Track document freshness; stale knowledge is worse than no knowledge.
